concepts.title: Running a Campaign: Keywords, Ad Groups, Copy, Landing Pages

pretest.prompt: You're setting up your first Google Ads campaign. Before reading on, sketch the structure: what sits at the top of the hierarchy, what sits underneath, and why doesn't 'one campaign with every keyword dumped in' work? Which element of the setup most strongly determines Quality Score, and why?
pretest.modelAnswer: Hierarchy: Campaign (budget + high-level goal) → Ad Groups (tight clusters of related keywords + matched ads) → Keywords + Ads + Landing Page per group. 'One campaign, all keywords' fails because ad relevance — and therefore Quality Score — depends on the match between the keyword the user typed, the ad copy they see, and the landing page they arrive at. A single ad cannot be simultaneously relevant to 'buy sneakers,' 'running shoes review,' and 'cheap slippers.' Splitting keywords into tight ad groups of 5-20 related terms lets each group have tailored ad copy and a matched landing page, which directly drives Quality Score up. Landing page match — what the page's H1 and lead content say relative to the query — is probably the most underweighted lever; it affects both Quality Score and conversion rate, compounding.

lesson.markdown:
## The campaign hierarchy

Google Ads organizes work in a three-level hierarchy, and getting the structure right is where most campaigns make or break their Quality Score before any bidding begins.

1. **Campaign** — the top level, where you set budget, geographic targeting, device targeting, and high-level goals (e.g., 'drive signups'). Most startups run one campaign per distinct audience or product line.
2. **Ad Groups** — tight clusters of closely related keywords inside a campaign. Each ad group shares its ad copy, so keywords in the group should be similar enough that a single ad addresses all of them coherently.
3. **Keywords + Ads + Landing Pages** — the lowest level. Each ad group contains keywords, several ads, and points to one or a few landing pages.

The key structural discipline: **tight ad groups**. Keywords in an ad group should be specific enough that you can write an ad where the headline contains (or closely mirrors) the query. If your ad group contains 200 keywords ranging from 'SaaS CRM' to 'email marketing,' no single ad can be relevant to all of them — CTR drops, Quality Score drops, CPC rises. A campaign with 30 tight ad groups of 5-15 keywords each dramatically outperforms one with 3 loose ad groups of 200 keywords each, even with the same keywords overall.

## Keyword research

Keyword discovery starts with Google's Keyword Planner (free, inside Google Ads): enter a seed term, see suggested keywords with monthly volume and competition scores. Other tools — SEMrush, Ahrefs, SpyFu — reveal which keywords your competitors are bidding on and how their ads rank.

The workflow: start with 5-10 seed terms describing your product. Expand each into 20-100 variants with modifiers (buyer-intent verbs: 'buy,' 'get,' 'download,' 'try'; specificity qualifiers: size, color, brand, price; negative qualifiers: 'not,' 'without,' 'except'). Sort the resulting list by expected commercial intent. The first keywords you actually bid on should be the high-intent long tail (previous concept's lesson); save the broader head terms for later when you have Quality Score foundations established.

Archives.com started with ~100,000 keywords and pruned down to the ~50,000 that actually converted profitably. This isn't a typical startup scale, but the pattern — cast wide in research, prune aggressively based on performance data — is the right shape at any scale.

## Ad copy that converts

Inside each ad group, write at least two ads so Google can rotate and compare them. A strong ad has four properties:

- **The keyword in the headline**, ideally verbatim. 'Buy leather shoes' as a headline for the keyword 'buy leather shoes' works dramatically better than 'Shop our collection.' The user's eye moves to the match; CTR and Quality Score both rise.
- **Dynamic Keyword Insertion** (DKI) is Google's feature for automating headline-keyword matching across many variant keywords in an ad group. Useful at scale; needs careful setup to avoid awkward insertions.
- **A specific value proposition** in the body — price, free trial, unique benefit, differentiator. 'Free shipping over $50' beats 'high quality.' Specific numbers and guarantees outperform adjectives.
- **A clear call to action** — 'Shop now,' 'Start free trial,' 'Get a quote.' Tell the user exactly what happens next.

Write at least two variants per ad group and let Google rotate them. Check which variant has higher CTR after ~500 impressions, pause the loser, write a new challenger.

## Landing pages that match

The landing page is the conversion venue and, equally important, a direct input into Quality Score. A landing page that matches the query signals to Google that the click is a good experience and to the user that they've arrived in the right place.

Four properties of a strong SEM landing page:

- **Match the query in the H1.** If the keyword is 'dental scheduling software' and the ad headline is 'Dental Scheduling Software — Built for Practices,' the landing page's H1 should be 'Dental Scheduling Software' not 'Welcome to DeskPilot.' Continuity raises conversion and Quality Score simultaneously.
- **One clear conversion action.** One primary CTA above the fold. Multiple CTAs dilute focus and drop conversion rate.
- **Minimal form fields.** Ask only for what's strictly needed. Every added field drops conversion noticeably.
- **Fast loading, especially on mobile.** Google actively incorporates page speed into Quality Score. A slow page costs you both conversion and auction position.

The common failure mode: sending all SEM traffic to the homepage. A homepage, by construction, is generic — it has to work for visitors from every channel. Dedicated landing pages per ad group (or per high-value keyword cluster) consistently out-convert homepage traffic by 2-5× and simultaneously raise Quality Score.

## Tracking with URL builders

Use Google Analytics URL Builder (or UTM tagging) to add `utm_source`, `utm_medium`, `utm_campaign`, and `utm_term` parameters to each ad's destination URL. This lets you see in analytics not just that a user came from Google, but which specific campaign, ad group, ad, and keyword drove them — and, critically, which ones *converted*. Without this, you can track clicks but not conversions back to their source, which means you can't compute real CPA per keyword or ad. Tracking is what makes every other discipline in this chapter possible.

workedExample.setup: Marcus is building AccuBooks, a $49/month bookkeeping SaaS for independent contractors. He's setting up his first Google Ads campaign with a $2,000/month budget. He starts with a sprawling keyword list of 400 terms.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — Campaign-level structure.** Marcus creates one campaign: 'AccuBooks-Contractors.' Budget $2,000/month, geo US + Canada, desktop and mobile. One goal: free trial signups.

**Step 2 — Ad group clustering.** He clusters his 400 keywords into 12 ad groups based on linguistic tightness:
- *Bookkeeping software for contractors* (15 keywords)
- *Freelance bookkeeping* (12 keywords)
- *Independent contractor accounting* (10 keywords)
- *1099 bookkeeping* (8 keywords)
- *Self-employed expense tracking* (14 keywords)
- ... 7 more
Each ad group contains only keywords that can be addressed by a single ad headline.

**Step 3 — Ad copy per ad group.** For *Bookkeeping software for contractors*:
- Ad A headline: 'Bookkeeping Software for Contractors'
- Body: 'Track expenses, send invoices, file 1099s. $49/mo, free 30-day trial.'
- CTA: 'Start Free Trial'
- Ad B (variant): 'Contractor Bookkeeping — Built Simple'
- Body: 'Designed for 1099 contractors. Expense tracking + invoicing. No credit card.'
- CTA: 'Try Free for 30 Days'

He writes similarly tailored pairs for the other 11 ad groups.

**Step 4 — Landing pages per ad group.** Instead of sending all 12 groups to his homepage, Marcus builds three dedicated landing pages:
- `/bookkeeping-for-contractors` — H1 'Bookkeeping Software for Contractors,' three-field signup (email + password + business type), social proof from independent contractors.
- `/freelance-accounting` — similar structure, reworded for freelance vocabulary.
- `/1099-bookkeeping` — tax-forward H1 and benefit framing.

Each ad group is mapped to the best-matching landing page.

**Step 5 — Tracking.** Every ad's URL includes `utm_source=google`, `utm_medium=cpc`, `utm_campaign=contractors`, `utm_content={ad_group}`, `utm_term={keyword}`. Conversion tracking is installed on the signup-complete page. Within Google Ads, he imports Google Analytics goals so he can see CPA per ad group, per ad, per keyword.

**Step 6 — First month results.** Budget pacing: Marcus caps daily spend at $65 to stay within the $2k monthly budget. Halfway through the month he checks:
- *Bookkeeping software for contractors* — Quality Score 8, CPC $3.20, conversion 4.5%, CPA $71. Strong.
- *Freelance bookkeeping* — Quality Score 6, CPC $4.80, conversion 2.1%, CPA $228. Weak — landing page match weaker than he thought.
- *1099 bookkeeping* — Quality Score 9, CPC $1.80, conversion 5.2%, CPA $35. Star performer — precise tax framing resonates.
- *Self-employed expense tracking* — QS 4, CPC $5.50, conversion 0.8%, CPA $687. Terrible — searchers are looking for a different product (expense-tracking apps, not bookkeeping).

**Step 7 — Optimization actions.** Marcus:
1. Pauses the *Self-employed expense tracking* ad group — intent mismatch; no amount of tuning will fix.
2. Adds 'expense tracking app,' 'mileage tracker,' 'receipt scanner' to his negative keyword list campaign-wide.
3. Rebuilds the *Freelance bookkeeping* landing page to match the vocabulary more tightly (H1: 'Freelance Bookkeeping Software,' not 'Accounting for Freelancers').
4. Reallocates the freed budget to *1099 bookkeeping* and *Bookkeeping software for contractors*, which are clear winners.

**What went right.** Tight ad groups → dedicated landing pages → ad copy matching both → UTM tracking. This is the core hygiene that makes every downstream optimization legible. Without it, he'd be looking at a single CPA number and unable to tell that *1099 bookkeeping* was a winner hiding inside an average campaign.

contrast.explanation: Keyword intent and match types (previous concept) are about *which keywords to pick*. Running a campaign (this concept) is about *how to organize them into structure that performs*. Tight ad groups + matched ad copy + matched landing pages are the mechanics that turn a correctly-chosen keyword into an actual low CAC. Picking the right keyword and then dumping it into a loose ad group with generic copy and a homepage destination wastes most of the advantage.

flashcards.front: State the three-level campaign hierarchy and what sits at each level.
flashcards.back: Campaign (budget, geo, goal) → Ad Groups (tight clusters of closely related keywords + shared ad copy) → Keywords + Ads + Landing Pages. The structural rule is: keywords in an ad group must be similar enough that one ad copy addresses all of them coherently. Loose ad groups tank Quality Score.

flashcards.front: Name the four properties of a strong SEM ad.
flashcards.back: (1) The keyword in the headline, ideally verbatim (drives CTR and Quality Score). (2) A specific value proposition in the body (numbers and guarantees beat adjectives). (3) A clear, unambiguous call to action. (4) At least two variants running simultaneously so you can A/B and rotate.

flashcards.front: The single most-overlooked lever in SEM campaign structure is the ______ page — it needs to echo the ______ in its H1, load fast (especially on mobile), have one clear CTA, and minimize ______ fields.
flashcards.back: landing ; query (or keyword) ; form — because it affects Quality Score AND conversion rate, a single landing-page improvement compounds through both the auction math and the downstream funnel.

flashcards.front: You send all SEM traffic to your homepage. Your Quality Scores are stuck at 4-5 despite decent ad copy. What's the likeliest explanation?
flashcards.back: The homepage is generic by design — it can't match any specific query closely. Google reads the landing-page relevance signal as weak, which caps Quality Score. Dedicated landing pages per ad group (with the keyword in the H1) typically lift Quality Score by 2-4 points and double conversion rate simultaneously.

flashcards.front: Why is UTM tagging with Google Analytics URL Builder essential for SEM work?
flashcards.back: Because without it, you can track that traffic came from Google but not which campaign, ad group, ad, or keyword drove each conversion. Without that attribution, you can't compute CPA per keyword — so you can't prune losers, scale winners, or make evidence-based bid decisions. Tagging turns SEM from a budget-level metric into a keyword-level optimization system.

scenarios.setup: A founder has one campaign, one ad group containing 300 mixed keywords, two generic ads ('Best Product Ever'), and all clicks go to the homepage. CTR is 1.1%, Quality Scores average 3/10, CAC is 4× the viable threshold.
scenarios.question: What's the root cause, and what's the specific sequence of fixes?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Root cause: the campaign is structurally one-size-fits-all. 300 keywords in one ad group means no ad can be relevant to most of them; Quality Score is weak across the board; CTR drops; CPC rises; CAC balloons. The fix sequence, high-to-low leverage: (1) Cluster the 300 keywords into 15-25 tight ad groups, each containing 10-20 closely related keywords a single ad can address. (2) Write at least two ads per ad group where the headline contains (or nearly matches) the keyword, with a specific value prop and a clear CTA. (3) Build 3-5 dedicated landing pages (one per major keyword cluster) with the keyword in the H1, one CTA above the fold, minimal form fields, and fast load. (4) Implement UTM tagging so every ad's clicks are attributable per keyword. (5) Install conversion tracking end-to-end. (6) Run for 2-3 weeks, then prune ad groups with CPA above threshold and scale ad groups where CAC is working. Expect Quality Score to rise from 3 to 7+ over 2-4 weeks, CPC to drop 40-60%, conversion rate to roughly double, and CAC to fall to under the viable threshold. This isn't a tuning problem — it's a structural reconstruction.
rubric: Identifies loose ad-group structure as the root cause
Prescribes the ad-group clustering step first
Addresses ad copy, landing pages, and tracking
Provides an expected outcome range (Quality Score lift, CPC drop)
Frames it as structural reconstruction, not parameter tuning

scenarios.setup: A founder asks: 'Should I write one ad per ad group and use Dynamic Keyword Insertion, or should I write manual ads tailored to each ad group?'
scenarios.question: When does each approach make sense, and what's the risk of DKI if not managed carefully?
scenarios.modelAnswer: DKI (Dynamic Keyword Insertion) is a Google feature that inserts the triggered keyword into the ad headline automatically. It's useful when you have many keywords in an ad group and maintaining hand-written variants for each is impractical — it gives you the 'keyword-in-headline' benefit at scale without writing a separate ad per keyword. Risk: DKI will happily insert awkward or inappropriate keyword text if your ad group contains misspellings, negative-sentiment queries, or grammatically incompatible phrases. 'Buy cheap JUNK SOFTWARE' as a generated headline because a user typed that query will not endear you to prospects. For tight ad groups with clean, similar keywords, hand-written ads tend to outperform DKI because you control tone and specificity. For large-scale campaigns where you can't practically write 50 ad variants, DKI is the pragmatic answer — paired with very careful keyword hygiene (no typos, no negative-sentiment terms, no grammatically awkward variants) and negative keywords that filter out the queries you don't want DKI to echo. General rule: start with hand-written ads for your highest-value 10-20 ad groups where care pays off, and use DKI for long-tail ad groups at the margin.
rubric: Explains what DKI does and when it's useful
Names the awkward-insertion risk concretely
Prescribes keyword hygiene as the mitigation
Offers the hand-written-high-value + DKI-long-tail split
Shows judgment about when each tool fits

teachback.prompt: Teach a founder how to structure and launch their first real SEM campaign. Cover: (1) the campaign/ad-group/keyword hierarchy and why tight ad groups matter, (2) the four properties of strong ad copy, (3) the four properties of strong landing pages and why homepages underperform, (4) UTM tagging and why tracking is a prerequisite for every optimization.
rubric: Names the hierarchy and the tight-ad-group principle
Lists the four ad-copy properties with at least one concrete example
Lists the four landing-page properties with the homepage-anti-pattern
Explains UTM tagging's role in attribution
Connects structure to Quality Score mechanism from the earlier concept

concepts.title: Testing SEM Cheaply: A Few Ads, a Few Hundred Dollars, Pre-Set Thresholds

pretest.prompt: You're running a Bullseye test on SEM as one of your three inner-ring channels. You have $500 and three weeks. Before reading on: what specifically are you trying to learn, how many ads would you run, and what's the single pre-committed number you'd set before launching that decides pass/fail?
pretest.modelAnswer: You're trying to learn three things: (1) approximate CPA for your product at small scale, (2) whether the CPA sits in a range that gives a viable LTV:CAC at full scale (with some allowance for Quality Score improvements over time), and (3) whether the channel can plausibly produce enough volume to matter. Run a small number of ads — the Inflection case study's recommendation is as few as four ads covering different messages, demographics, or landing pages — targeting high-intent long-tail keywords. Pre-commit the pass/fail number: 'CPA under $X after three weeks and with a clear path to $Y.' The specific $X depends on the business's LTV, but the discipline is writing it down before you see any data. Ambiguous threshold = automatic no (ch02 cheap-testing principle).

lesson.markdown:
## Why SEM is unusually easy to test cheaply

Compared to viral (which requires engineering a loop before any measurement) or sales (which requires building a pipeline of conversations), SEM is close to a plug-and-play test. You can go from 'no campaign' to 'live ads serving real traffic' in an afternoon. You can spend $500 over a few weeks and emerge with real data on CPC, CTR, conversion rate, and CPA for your product against actual customers.

The Archives.com case study in this chapter is explicit: the team's testing philosophy was 'learn as much as we could for as little cost as possible.' They ran small campaigns before they built the product, using ads and landing pages to test messaging, feature framing, and which customer segments responded. The output wasn't just a yes/no on SEM — it was a dataset shaping the product itself.

## A sensible test design

- **Budget: $500-2,000.** The lower end ($500) is enough to produce directional data on whether your CPA is in the ballpark of viable. The higher end ($2,000) gives you enough volume to distinguish good keywords from bad ones and test a couple of landing page variants. Past that range, you're no longer testing — you're scaling, which is the Focus step, not the Test step.
- **Duration: 2-4 weeks.** Shorter than that and you're not seeing enough conversion data for CPA to be trustworthy. Longer than that and you're drifting into commitment without explicit commitment.
- **Number of ads: four is enough.** Matthew Monahan's explicit advice in the chapter: start with about four ads. Four is enough to test different messages, different target demographics, and different landing pages, while small enough that each ad gets enough impressions to produce a statistically meaningful CTR.
- **Keyword selection: long-tail, high commercial intent only.** Don't start on head terms — CPC is too high to learn cheaply. Pick 20-100 long-tail transactional keywords where CPCs are under $2-3 and conversion intent is clear.
- **Negative keywords from day one.** Even in a small test, a ten-term negative list prevents broad-match bleed from distorting your initial data.
- **Conversion tracking installed before launch.** This is non-negotiable. A test without conversion tracking measures clicks, not customers — the wrong layer (see the ch09-sem-definition scenarios).

## The Archives.com style of test: message as variable

Inflection's most interesting move was treating ads as a *product-research instrument*, not just a customer-acquisition instrument. They ran simultaneous ads testing fundamentally different headlines:

- 'Discover as many of your ancestors as possible.'
- 'Trace your family tree as far back as possible.'
- 'Find out if you're related to a celebrity.'

Each headline implied a different product. By measuring CTR and downstream conversion on each, they learned not just *whether* SEM could acquire customers, but *which product direction* customers responded to — before they built the product. This is SEM as pre-build discovery, a pattern the book is explicit about: build traction and product development in parallel.

## Pre-committed pass/fail thresholds

From the ch02 cheap-testing principle: write down what 'pass' and 'fail' mean before launching. For SEM specifically, good threshold shapes:

- **'Pass'**: CPA under $X after three weeks, with at least one ad group showing CPA clearly below $X and Quality Score 6+. The $X is your LTV / 3 (or whatever your cushion choice is). 'At least one clear winner' matters because it proves the channel has room to concentrate on its winners, not just that the average works.
- **'Break-even-ish'**: CPA at or near your threshold — close enough that Quality Score improvements, landing-page iteration, or long-tail expansion could plausibly push it into 'pass' territory over the next cycle. Treated as a 'yellow' — continue optimizing, don't yet scale.
- **'Fail'**: CPA multiples above your threshold, or dominant weakness in Quality Score despite tight structure. Accept that SEM is not your channel this cycle. Demote and reallocate. Do not keep optimizing a structurally unfit campaign; that's the trap of sunk cost.

The key property: ambiguous thresholds ('we'll see what happens,' 'we'll know when we see it') are effectively 'no' — they let the team rationalize keeping an underperforming channel alive because there's no commitment point.

## What the test doesn't tell you

A cheap SEM test answers two questions: (a) does your CPA at small scale sit where your LTV can support it? (b) is there a plausible path from 'unoptimized' to 'optimized' within reach? It does *not* tell you what happens at 10× volume (competition may harden, long-tail exhausts, Quality Score dynamics change), what CPAs look like in a year as the market evolves, or whether the customers you acquired via SEM retain at the same rates as customers from other channels. Those are commitment-stage questions, not test-stage questions.

## Budget pacing within the test

Set a daily budget cap that respects your total test spend: $500 over 21 days = ~$24/day. Google will pace within your cap. If one keyword or ad group is burning budget fast without converting, pause it mid-test — waiting for the whole three weeks to confirm what two days of data already showed wastes learning capital. The test can have its own micro-iterations inside the overall window.

workedExample.setup: Emma runs a handmade pet-collar ecommerce site, LeashLab. $45 per collar, 55% gross margin, ~30% of first-time buyers come back within 12 months for another collar (so LTV ≈ $45 × 1.3 × 55% = $32). She has $600 to test SEM as one of her Bullseye inner-ring channels.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — Compute the pass/fail threshold.** LTV = $32. At a 3:1 target, sustainable CAC is ~$11. Writing it down: 'Pass if CPA under $11 after three weeks, with at least one ad group under $8 and Quality Score 6+.'

**Step 2 — Keywords.** Emma picks 40 long-tail, high-intent keywords, all exact or phrase match:
- 'handmade dog collar'
- 'leather pet collar'
- 'custom dog collar small breed'
- 'personalized leather dog collar'
- ... 36 more

She deliberately avoids 'dog collar' (too broad) and 'dog' (way too broad). Expected CPC: $0.50-$1.50.

**Step 3 — Four ads.** She groups the keywords into three ad groups (handmade, leather, personalized) and writes four ads total across them, each with the keyword in the headline, a specific value prop ('Free engraving,' '30-day money back'), and a clear CTA ('Design yours').

**Step 4 — Landing page.** She builds one dedicated landing page (`/custom-collars`) with H1 'Handmade Leather Dog Collars,' a single large CTA ('Design Yours'), a 2-field signup (email + dog size), and fast-loading photos. Her normal ecommerce homepage would have worked worse — too many competing CTAs.

**Step 5 — Negative keywords.** Before launching: 'free,' 'coupon,' 'wholesale,' 'training,' 'electric,' 'shock,' 'cat,' 'flea,' 'wikipedia,' 'reddit.'

**Step 6 — Daily budget.** $600 / 21 days ≈ $28.50/day cap.

**Step 7 — Launch and monitor.**
- Week 1: 180 clicks at $1.20 CPC = $216. 6 purchases. CPA = $36. Well above threshold. But Quality Score average is 7/10 and conversion rate is 3.3% — which is low. She checks her landing page: the product photos are slow to load on mobile. She fixes it.
- Week 2: 210 clicks at $1.10 CPC = $231. 11 purchases. CPA = $21. Improving but still above threshold. The 'handmade dog collar' ad group is converting at 5%; the 'leather pet collar' ad group is converting at 2% — intent for the word 'pet' may be broader than her target (includes cats, reptiles, birds). She adds 'cat,' 'lizard,' 'bird' to negatives and pauses the weakest keywords.
- Week 3: 120 clicks at $0.90 CPC = $108. 9 purchases. CPA = $12. Still just above the $11 threshold, but now the 'handmade dog collar' ad group alone shows CPA = $7.

**Step 8 — Test verdict.** By the pre-committed threshold: technically a marginal pass (CPA = $12 on aggregate is slightly above $11, but one ad group is at $7 with QS 8 — clear room to concentrate). Emma decides the data strongly suggests viability, and she commits a follow-up Focus-stage investment — scaling the 'handmade dog collar' ad group, building a dedicated landing page for it specifically, and expanding the long-tail within that category. Total test spend: $555. Data acquired: real CPA, a clear winner ad group, and a validated ICP signal (customers who type 'handmade' have specific purchase intent she can double down on).

**What she didn't do.** She didn't launch on broad match. She didn't use her homepage. She didn't skip conversion tracking. She didn't extend the test to six weeks hoping numbers would improve. She set a threshold, ran the test, accepted the data, made a clean decision.

contrast.explanation: Chapter 2's cheap-testing principles — speed over scale, pre-committed thresholds, small spend — apply to every channel. SEM is one of the friendliest channels to test cheaply because the infrastructure (the auction, the ad platform, the landing page) doesn't need to be built from scratch. The adaptations specific to SEM: four ads not dozens, long-tail only, negative keywords from day one, conversion tracking before launch. The ch02 principles tell you *what* test shape to run; this concept tells you *how* to execute that shape in SEM specifically.

flashcards.front: What's a reasonable SEM test budget, duration, and number of ads for a Bullseye-style test?
flashcards.back: Budget: $500-2,000. Duration: 2-4 weeks. Number of ads: about four — enough to test different messages, demographics, and landing pages while keeping each ad in enough impressions to produce meaningful CTR. The explicit source: Matthew Monahan (Inflection / Archives.com) recommends starting with roughly four ads.

flashcards.front: What's the single most important thing to pre-commit to before launching an SEM test, and why?
flashcards.back: A written pass/fail CPA threshold tied to your LTV (typically LTV / 3). Without it, the test's output is interpretable after the fact in whatever way keeps the channel alive; with it, the test is decisive. Ambiguous thresholds are effectively 'no' — they just take longer to recognize.

flashcards.front: For the first SEM test, keywords should be ______ (not head) with ______ (high commercial intent), because learning cheaply requires CPCs low enough that $500-2000 produces a meaningful number of clicks and conversions.
flashcards.back: long-tail ; transactional (or high commercial intent) — head terms are too expensive to learn from cheaply, and low-intent terms produce clicks that don't convert, distorting the CPA signal.

flashcards.front: A first SEM test produces a CPA of $25 against a sustainable CAC of $20. Is this a pass, fail, or yellow?
flashcards.back: Yellow — break-even-ish. The channel is close to threshold but not clearly over it. The correct move is to keep optimizing (Quality Score, landing page match, long-tail expansion) rather than scale or abandon. A follow-up cycle of optimization often pushes a yellow channel to green; acting on yellow as if it were green is premature scaling.

flashcards.front: What did Archives.com use their early SEM tests to learn, beyond whether SEM worked as a channel?
flashcards.back: They used SEM as a product-research instrument — testing different headlines (e.g., 'discover as many ancestors as possible' vs. 'trace your family tree as far back as possible' vs. 'related to a celebrity') to learn which product framings users responded to before investing in building the corresponding product. The channel produced product-direction evidence as well as customer data — traction and product development in parallel.

scenarios.setup: A founder wants to test SEM with a $5,000 budget over three weeks. He plans to launch 60 ads across 12 ad groups covering head terms, mid-tail, and long-tail, with all match types including broad match, and no negative keywords list. He wants to 'get as much volume as possible to find what works.'
scenarios.question: Diagnose what's wrong with this test design and propose a better one. What single change would give him the most additional learning per dollar?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Several problems. (1) $5k over three weeks is near the top end of test-budget range — he's closer to small-scale scaling than testing. (2) 60 ads across 12 ad groups means each ad will accumulate too few impressions to produce a reliable CTR signal — you want fewer, more-compared ads, not more, less-compared ads. (3) Broad match with no negatives will pull in huge volume of low-intent impressions that will silently destroy his CPA signal and waste a large fraction of the budget on irrelevant traffic. (4) Testing head terms simultaneously with mid- and long-tail conflates three very different economic regimes and makes attribution muddy. Better design: (a) cut to ~4-8 ads across 3-4 tight ad groups on long-tail high-intent keywords only, (b) exact and phrase match only, (c) build a 30-term negative list before launching, (d) cap daily spend at $80 so $5k spans the full three weeks, (e) install conversion tracking with UTM tagging end-to-end, (f) pre-commit the CPA threshold. Single highest-leverage change: **add a negative keyword list and turn off broad match.** That alone will probably cut wasted spend by 30-50% and produce cleaner CPA data. Without it, his test results will be about 'SEM with broad match bleed' rather than 'SEM done well.' Negative keywords are the single discipline with the most learning-per-dollar impact in a first test.
rubric: Identifies that 60 ads dilutes signal per ad
Identifies broad match + no negatives as the dominant leak

scenarios.setup: A first SEM test concludes. CPA is $14 against a sustainable CAC of $15. The founder's team is split — one camp says 'barely passed, scale aggressively'; another says 'too close to threshold, kill it.'
scenarios.question: What's the correct call and why? What should they do in the next 2-4 weeks?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Neither extreme is right. Test-stage CPA at or just under threshold is a *yellow* signal, not a green one — it says the channel is viable but unoptimized. The correct move is continuation, not aggressive scaling. Reasoning: (1) CPA at test stage reflects an unoptimized campaign, so most channels have meaningful headroom for CPA improvement through Quality Score gains, landing page iteration, negative-keyword expansion, and long-tail concentration. A $14 CPA today could plausibly be $8-10 CPA next month without changing bids or budget. (2) Aggressive scaling at $14 CPA with a $15 ceiling leaves no cushion — a 10% LTV error, a small conversion-rate dip, or new competition pushes the channel into loss territory immediately. (3) Killing it ignores the fact that the test passed its threshold, barely but legitimately. Correct plan for weeks 4-7: (a) concentrate spend on the single best-performing ad group from the test, (b) build a dedicated landing page for that ad group, (c) aggressively expand negatives, (d) work Quality Score through ad copy iteration and keyword/landing-page alignment, (e) hold total spend roughly flat so you're optimizing rather than scaling, (f) re-measure at week 7. If CPA is clearly below threshold with cushion, then scale. If still hovering at threshold, extend another cycle or demote the channel. This is the 'break-even-ish → work on it before scaling' discipline from the testing lesson.
rubric: Identifies the result as yellow (not green or red)
Prescribes optimization cycle before scaling
Names specific optimization levers (Quality Score, landing page, negatives)
Warns against scaling without CPA cushion
Sets a clear re-check point with decision rules

scenarios.setup: A founder wants to test SEM for $400 over two weeks for his $9/month freemium mobile app.
scenarios.question: Is this a reasonable test? What would you expect to learn, and what wouldn't you?
scenarios.modelAnswer: It's a reasonable diagnostic, not a definitive test. $400 over two weeks at reasonable CPCs for consumer-mobile keywords (say $1-2) produces perhaps 200-400 clicks. At freemium conversion rates (1-3% to paid), that's 2-12 paying users — a sample too small to produce a stable CPA estimate. What he *can* learn: (a) whether the auction mechanics and Quality Score work for his keyword space, (b) rough CPC range for his chosen keywords, (c) whether landing-page click-through-to-install behaves as expected, (d) whether there's obvious intent mismatch in his keyword list. What he *can't* learn: (a) a trustworthy CPA number (sample too small), (b) whether Quality Score improvements will eventually bring CAC into sustainable range, (c) long-term retention of SEM-acquired users. Better framing: treat the $400 as a Stage-0 feasibility check — does the channel mechanically work for us and is the CPC in a plausible range? If yes, run a proper $1,500-2,000 test in the next cycle for a real pass/fail reading. If the stage-0 check reveals CPCs so high that sustainable CPA is out of reach even with best Quality Score (the freemium app wall), he saves the $1,500 and moves to other channels.
rubric: Identifies $400 as diagnostic scale, not definitive
Names what can be learned at that scale
Names what can't be learned (sample-size CPA issue)
Proposes staged testing (diagnostic → proper test)
Notes the freemium-economics wall specifically

concepts.title: Common SEM Pitfalls and Quality Score Spirals

pretest.prompt: Before reading on — list three specific ways a startup can burn money on Google Ads faster than the founder notices. For each, describe the feedback loop that makes the problem compound rather than stabilize.
pretest.modelAnswer: Three common ways: (1) Broad match with no negatives — the campaign serves on progressively broader, less-relevant queries, CPA degrades silently, Quality Score drops, CPC rises, producing a negative spiral. (2) Bidding only on brand terms (your own company name) — this looks profitable in a report but doesn't add incremental customers, since those searchers would have reached you via organic results anyway. The CAC you see is almost entirely an attribution artifact. (3) No conversion tracking — you optimize to CTR or CPC, which makes the ads 'look good' but has no guarantee of improving CPA; the measurement is on the wrong layer, so every decision is disconnected from what matters. All three compound because the feedback data you're reading is systematically wrong about whether the campaign is working, so 'optimizations' often make things worse.

lesson.markdown:
## The predictable failure modes

SEM has a specific catalog of ways teams lose money — not because the channel is broken, but because specific disciplines get dropped, usually together. Every pitfall in this concept is avoidable with hygiene already covered in earlier concepts; they show up here as failure modes because they're predictable, frequent, and expensive.

### 1. Bidding only on your brand name

A common early-campaign move: a founder buys ads on their own company name ('AcmeCRM') and sees 'great' numbers — high CTR, low CPC, high conversion. The CPA looks spectacular.

The problem: almost every one of those clicks would have reached you via organic results anyway. The searcher typed your brand name — they already know who you are. Your ad spend intercepts traffic you'd have received for free and assigns the customer to 'SEM' in your attribution. Your reported CAC is an illusion; your *incremental* CAC from brand bidding is often near-infinite because you're not actually acquiring customers you wouldn't otherwise have acquired.

When brand bidding makes sense: when competitors are bidding on your brand name (forcing defensive bidding to keep them out of top placement), or when your brand term is ambiguous with a generic term. When it doesn't: as a primary channel. A campaign that looks great because 80% of its conversions are branded is measuring nothing real.

### 2. Broad match bleeding without negatives

Covered in the keyword concept but worth restating as a pitfall. Broad match + no negative keyword list is the fastest way to burn budget in SEM. You pay for clicks on queries you never intended to target, conversion rate craters, Quality Score drops because your CTR on the mismatched queries is low, CPC rises, CAC compounds upward.

The reason this specifically is a spiral: each of the three steps reinforces the others. Worse relevance → lower Quality Score → higher CPC → lower CTR on the next bidding cycle → even lower Quality Score. The campaign gets worse faster than the team notices, because the weekly report shows 'more spend, same clicks' rather than clearly flagging the relevance decay.

The fix is always the same: exact and phrase match as default, negative keyword list built before launch and maintained every week from the Search Terms report.

### 3. Quality Score spirals

The mirror image of the Quality Score compounding advantage from concept 2. A campaign that starts with weak relevance — loose ad groups, generic landing pages, mismatched ad copy — builds a low Quality Score baseline. Low Quality Score raises CPC, which means less traffic per budget dollar, which means fewer conversions, which means less optimization data, which slows every downstream improvement.

The spiral is especially brutal because at the early stages you often can't afford to push through it — you'd have to spend disproportionately to overcome the Quality Score drag and wait for CTR-history to rebuild, which takes weeks. Many startups abandon SEM after exactly this pattern, concluding 'SEM doesn't work for us,' when the actual diagnosis was 'we started with bad structure and the spiral punished us.'

Prevention is much cheaper than cure: start with tight ad groups, keyword-matched headlines, dedicated landing pages, and exact/phrase match from day one. Quality Score builds on the campaign-level, ad-group-level, and account-level, so early decisions set trajectories that are expensive to correct later.

### 4. Ignoring conversion tracking

You can run Google Ads without installing conversion tracking. If you do, you'll see clicks, CTR, and CPC, and you'll be able to 'optimize' — toward what? CTR optimization without conversion context makes ads more click-attractive, which may or may not make them more convert-attractive. CPC optimization without conversion context pursues cheaper clicks even when the cheaper clicks convert worse.

Without conversion tracking, you are optimizing a different thing than the thing you want to optimize. This shows up months later as 'we've been running ads for six months, we know CTR is up to 5%, and we still don't know if we're profitable.' The fix: install conversion tracking before launch, end-to-end, including server-side for high-value events. Every optimization decision should be readable as a CPA or LTV:CAC consequence.

### 5. Optimizing CPC instead of CPA

Related to the tracking pitfall. Even with conversion tracking installed, teams habitually look at CPC and bid adjustments as the primary lever. 'Our CPC went down 20% this month' is a common report. But CPC can drop because you won fewer auctions, because you shifted to less competitive keywords that also have lower intent, or because your Quality Score genuinely improved. Only the last one actually improves CAC; the first two make it worse.

The correct primary metric is CPA or LTV:CAC. Track CPC as a diagnostic; judge by CPA or ratio. A 'CPC went down' report paired with 'CPA also went down' is a win. A 'CPC went down' report paired with 'CPA went up' means the CPC drop came at the cost of intent — fix the keyword mix.

### 6. Scaling before optimizing (or the reverse: never scaling)

Two failure modes in opposite directions:

- **Premature scale.** A test shows CPA at threshold or slightly better. The team 10× the budget. Because the campaign wasn't yet optimized, the larger spend pulls in more marginal keywords with worse CPA, and the aggregate CPA degrades as scale rises. The test-stage result was the best the campaign was going to be without work; scaling before optimizing locks in the test-stage inefficiency and amplifies it.
- **Perpetual 'testing.'** The opposite: a channel clearly works, but the team keeps running small tests and never commits Focus-stage resources. After a year they've produced decent unit economics but zero meaningful volume. Bullseye's Focus step (ch02) exists specifically for this — once a channel has passed the test, double down on it until it saturates.

The discipline: optimize, then scale. Scale only after the channel has clear headroom and no more obvious structural improvements are left. And once it's working at scale, keep committing — don't leave a proven winner on a perpetual 'test' budget.

### 7. Content Network confusion

Google offers two ad networks: Search (the traditional SEM, ads next to search results) and Display/Content (ads on non-Google sites in the ad network). They use the same interface but behave very differently. The Content Network isn't intent capture — it's interruption targeting, closer to banner ads than to SEM. CTRs are much lower, conversion rates are much lower, and the skills and metrics that make Search SEM work don't transfer cleanly.

Beginners sometimes launch campaigns with both networks enabled (the default in some setups), see inflated impression counts and plummeting CTRs, and think Search SEM is broken. Fix: for SEM specifically, set the campaign to Search Network only. Explore Display/Content later as a separate channel with separate measurement and strategy, after Search is established and profitable.

## The common thread

Most SEM failures trace back to measuring the wrong layer, keeping wrong structure, or skipping the discipline loop (negatives, conversion tracking, threshold-driven decisions). The channel is well-engineered and mathematically legible — the pitfalls are human-side failures of discipline that the channel's own mechanics (Quality Score feedback, match-type breadth, attribution complexity) happen to amplify. The good news: every pitfall has a specific fix, and most are weekly-discipline problems rather than strategic ones.

workedExample.setup: Jordan has been running Google Ads for his $20/month B2B SaaS for four months. Monthly spend: $4,000. Reported CPA: $180. Reported LTV:CAC: 3:1 — technically passing threshold. Growth is slower than expected, and he's not sure why 'good' numbers aren't translating into real growth.
workedExample.walkthrough:
A friend walks Jordan's campaign structure and finds several simultaneous pitfalls.

**Pitfall 1 — Brand bidding.** 45% of Jordan's reported conversions come from his branded keyword ('JordanAppHQ'). CPA on branded = $12, conversion 22%. Strip out branded and the *non-branded* CAC is $370, not $180. His incremental CAC from real SEM acquisition is above LTV, not below. The reported 3:1 ratio is an artifact.

**Pitfall 2 — No negatives.** Jordan has 12 ad groups, most on broad match, with no negative keyword list. The Search Terms report reveals he's been serving ads on 'free accounting software,' 'accounting tutorials,' 'accounting jobs,' 'accounting internships,' 'CPA exam prep' — none of which are buyers of his B2B SaaS. Roughly 35% of his spend flowed through queries like these.

**Pitfall 3 — Quality Score spiral.** Because of the loose ad groups and broad match bleed, his average Quality Score is 4/10 despite the product being legitimately relevant to the right searchers. CPC on non-branded keywords averages $6.80 — roughly double what it would be at Quality Score 8.

**Pitfall 4 — No dedicated landing pages.** All 12 ad groups send traffic to his homepage, which has 5 competing CTAs, a carousel, and 4 form fields for signup. Conversion rate from ad click to paid signup: 1.2%. A dedicated landing page per ad group would almost certainly lift this to 3-5%.

**Pitfall 5 — Optimizing CPC.** Jordan has been adjusting bids weekly to chase lower CPC. His CPC has gone from $8.20 to $6.80 over four months. In the same period, his CPA has gone from $145 to $180 — CPC down, CPA up. He was optimizing the wrong metric.

**The fix plan.**
1. **Separate branded and non-branded.** Move branded keywords into their own campaign so the metrics aren't blended. Strip branded from aggregate CPA reports when evaluating SEM's real incremental contribution.
2. **Build a negative list.** From the Search Terms report, tag and negative-list ~80 poor-intent terms. Switch broad match to exact and phrase for the first month.
3. **Rebuild ad groups.** Cut 12 loose ad groups to 20 tight ones, each with 10-15 closely related keywords and tailored ad copy.
4. **Dedicated landing pages.** Build 4-5 landing pages matching the highest-value ad group clusters. Keyword-in-H1, single CTA, two-field signup.
5. **Switch to CPA-primary monitoring.** Weekly dashboard: CPA per ad group, Quality Score per ad group, LTV:CAC projection. CPC visible but only as a diagnostic.
6. **Re-evaluate at 6 weeks.** If non-branded CAC drops below sustainable threshold, scale. If still above, demote SEM to a secondary channel and redirect budget.

**Expected outcome.** After 6 weeks: Quality Score rises from 4 to 7, CPC drops from $6.80 to ~$3.50, conversion rate lifts from 1.2% to 3%. Non-branded CPA: $3.50 / 0.03 = $117 — now below his $200 sustainable CAC with real cushion. The 3:1 ratio becomes legitimate rather than artifactual.

Jordan's original problem wasn't that SEM didn't work for his product — it was that five simultaneous pitfalls were corrupting his measurement and hobbling his Quality Score. The channel was viable; the discipline wasn't. Most struggling SEM campaigns look like Jordan's.

contrast.explanation: Testing SEM (previous concept) is about generating an honest read on whether the channel works for your product. Pitfalls are the patterns that make even a decent-looking test or campaign lie to you about whether it's working. The relationship: a disciplined test avoids most pitfalls by construction (tight ad groups, conversion tracking, negative keywords, pre-committed threshold). A campaign that drifts from those disciplines during scale-up is where the pitfalls show up and where most SEM money is wasted.

flashcards.front: Why is bidding only on your brand name usually a misleading measure of SEM success?
flashcards.back: Because most of those clicks would have reached you via organic results anyway — the searcher already knows your brand. The reported CAC is an attribution artifact, not real incremental customer acquisition. Your true *incremental* CAC from brand bidding is often near-infinite; the headline number lies. Strip branded from aggregate reports to see whether SEM is actually adding customers.

flashcards.front: Describe the Quality Score spiral and why it's hard to recover from.
flashcards.back: Weak relevance → low CTR → low Quality Score → high CPC → fewer clicks per budget dollar → less data to optimize from → continued weak relevance. It compounds because each step feeds the next. Recovery requires spending disproportionately to push through the Quality Score drag while rebuilding CTR history — typically 3-6 weeks of structural work (tight ad groups, matched landing pages, pruned keywords) before the spiral reverses. Prevention is much cheaper than cure.

flashcards.front: Optimizing ______ alone is a common failure mode even when conversion tracking exists — it can drop because you won fewer auctions or moved to lower-______ keywords, both of which make ______ worse, not better.
flashcards.back: CPC ; intent ; CPA — the correct primary metric is CPA (or LTV:CAC); CPC is only a diagnostic.

flashcards.front: You launched a campaign with Search + Display Network both enabled (a common default). Your impression count is huge but your CTR is 0.3% and conversion is near zero. What's the likely explanation?
flashcards.back: The Display/Content Network is serving most of your impressions, and it works very differently from Search. Display is interruption-based banner-style advertising — CTRs are typically 10-20× lower than Search, conversion is much lower, and the audience is not self-selecting via search intent. For SEM specifically, set the campaign to Search Network only. Treat Display as a separate channel with its own strategy and measurement.

flashcards.front: Name the two opposing scaling failure modes and the discipline that addresses both.
flashcards.back: (1) Premature scale — 10× budget on a just-passing test; the unoptimized campaign degrades as scale pulls in marginal keywords with worse CPA. (2) Perpetual testing — never committing Focus-stage resources to a proven channel, so it never produces meaningful volume. The discipline: optimize before scaling (not during), and once the optimized channel is clearly profitable with headroom, commit fully rather than leaving it on a test budget.

scenarios.setup: A founder presents her SEM dashboard: total spend $10k/month, total CAC $80 — well under her $200 sustainable CAC. She's about to scale to $30k/month.
scenarios.question: What specific questions should she answer before scaling 3×? Name the pitfalls she should check for.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Before scaling, check for each of the reported-CAC-is-an-illusion pitfalls. (1) What percentage of conversions come from branded keywords? If 30%+ of her CAC is inflated by brand-name bidding where she'd have won those users organically, her true incremental CAC is much higher than $80. Strip brand and recompute. (2) Is her campaign on broad match? Check the Search Terms report — is she serving on queries she didn't intend? (3) What's her Quality Score distribution? If it's in the 4-6 range, she has substantial CPC headroom she hasn't captured yet — scaling before optimizing locks in the current CPC. (4) What's her conversion tracking coverage? Are all conversion events captured, including server-side for trial-to-paid? A partial tracking setup can under-count conversions and make CAC look worse than it is, or miss events that matter (refunds, churn). (5) Optimizing CPC or CPA? If she's been chasing CPC down, her CAC improvement might be partly an illusion hiding worse quality. (6) Mix of head vs. long-tail? Scaling often pushes the mix toward head terms as long-tail saturates, changing CAC. (7) Payback period at 3× scale — does cash flow support it? Also: scale gradually — 2× first for 2-3 weeks, evaluate CAC drift, then 3× if it holds. Jumping 3× hides which of the above factors caused any degradation.
rubric: Names the branded-vs-non-branded split check
Names broad match / negatives check
Names Quality Score headroom check
Names conversion tracking completeness check
Recommends gradual scaling rather than jumping 3×
Touches payback-period / cash-flow consideration

scenarios.setup: A team concludes 'SEM doesn't work for us' after 3 months and $15k of spend. Their campaign: broad match, one homepage landing page for all ad groups, 2 generic ads per ad group, no negatives, no conversion tracking for the first month. They're about to abandon the channel.
scenarios.question: Based on the pitfalls described, is their conclusion correct? What would you recommend?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Their conclusion is almost certainly wrong — they didn't test SEM, they tested a specifically broken execution of SEM. Every pitfall from the lesson is present: broad match without negatives (spending on irrelevant queries), homepage landing (weak Quality Score and conversion), generic ads (weak CTR, weak Quality Score), no conversion tracking for the first month (can't even measure CPA reliably for a third of the period). The channel they ran was one where the auction punished them systemically; the channel they *could* have run — tight ad groups, matched landing pages, exact/phrase + negatives, conversion tracking end-to-end — was never tested. Recommendation: (a) don't abandon SEM yet; (b) rebuild the campaign cleanly following the concepts from this chapter — tight ad groups on long-tail transactional keywords, exact and phrase match with 50+ negative keywords, dedicated landing pages for the top 3-5 ad groups, conversion tracking before launch, pre-committed CPA threshold; (c) run a proper $1,500-2,000 test over 3 weeks; (d) decide from that data. If they had tested SEM honestly in month 1 and it failed, the conclusion would be 'SEM isn't our channel' — legitimate. What they actually tested was 'SEM without discipline,' which fails for almost everyone regardless of product fit. The broken version isn't informative about the channel.
rubric: Identifies that the failure is executional, not channel-fit
Names multiple pitfalls from the lesson that apply
Distinguishes 'SEM tested properly and failed' from 'SEM run badly'
Prescribes a clean rebuild with specific structural fixes
Recommends a second honest test before concluding

teachback.prompt: Teach a founder the common ways SEM campaigns waste money.
